text.title: Accessibility Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns commonly observed in shipped UIs. Each pairs symptom + root-cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: `<div onclick>` triggers the action on mouse but not on Enter / Space; screen-reader announces "clickable" instead of "button".
antipattern.root-cause: Author bypassed native HTML because of styling concerns; never added role/tabindex/keypress handler.
antipattern.fix: Replace with `<button type="button">`; remove role and tabindex. Style with CSS, not by changing the element. See rule semantic-first.

antipattern.symptom: `role="button"` on a `<button>`; `aria-label="Search"` on a `<label>Search</label> <input>`. AT announces twice; users hear noise.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treats ARIA as defensive armour instead of patch-on-gap.
antipattern.fix: Strip redundant ARIA; keep only attributes that fill a real semantic gap (live regions, aria-expanded on disclosures). See rule aria-as-last-resort.

antipattern.symptom: Tab enters a custom modal; Tab again leaves the page entirely; user cannot return to content.
antipattern.root-cause: Custom modal lacks focus-trap during open and focus-return on close.
antipattern.fix: Implement focus-trap (return tab from last focusable to first within modal); on close, return focus to the trigger. See rule keyboard-completeness.

antipattern.symptom: Form placeholders use `color: #aaa` on white background (2.3:1); users with low vision cannot read them.
antipattern.root-cause: Designer treated placeholder as decoration; placeholder is informational text.
antipattern.fix: Use a labelled input with visible label; if a placeholder remains, raise contrast to >= 4.5:1 or rely on the label for the information. See rule contrast-floor.
